description: Apollo 10 Flight Journal - Day 5, part 19: 'We is down among them'

tokens: The DOI burn occurred whilst the LM was behind the Moon.
Data recording was in low bit rate, so detailed data on the DPS performance was not obtained.
The result of the manoeuvre indicates that the DPS performance was very close to nominal.
The burn was initiated at 13.1 per cent throttle setting and the throttle was advanced to 40 per cent at approximately 14 seconds until its conclusion.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: LM orbit following DOI is resolved by CSM VHF ranging.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is getting an idea of the time remaining to the phasing burn.
The LMSnoopyshould pass 100° east longitude, 33:31 prior to the phasing burn ignition.
This information was passed up to the crew as part of the phasing burn PAD.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Phasing burn is performed with the DPS engine.
This posigrade manoeuvre is used to establish a CSM lead angle equivalent to that which occurs at the cut-off of a nominal powered ascent from the lunar surface.

This manoeuvre elevates the LM apolune to approximately 195 nm (361 km), placing the LM in a 'dwell' orbit to allow the CSM to overtake and be in the correct relative position at the time of the LM insertion burn.
At the time of the phasing burn, the CSM is 9 degrees behind the LM and at the insertion burn it will be 16 degrees in front.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: P30 is used to load the ignition time and the required Delta-V in each axis into the LGC, for burns of the DPS, APS and some RCS manoeuvres.
This load is for the LM phasing burn.
Once the TIGand Delta-V have been loaded, the LGC will flash Verb 06 Noun 42 and display what it expects the resultant orbit will be following this manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is reading out the phasing burn ignition time from the PAD Duke read up to him on the previous revolution.
Stafford inputs this time as part of the P30 setup.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is the P30 prediction for the resultant orbit in nautical miles following a nominal phasing burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The phasing PAD informed the crew that the LMSnoopywill pass over landing site 2, 10:30 prior to the phasing burn ignition.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Flight Plan calls for the LM to pitch down 90 degrees at phasing minus 47 minutes, and then to yaw left 180 degrees at phasing minus 42 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is looking for the Hasselblad camera.
Cernan reminds him that he was the last to use it, when he was photographing the CSM during station keeping.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan has set the Maurer 16-mm sequence camera running at 6 frames per second as they make their low pass.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young is estimating the LMSnoopy, to be 14 nautical miles [26 km] below the CSMCharlie Brownat this point.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: See explanation of the error at099:43:34.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Verb 83 is used to initiate routine 31 which displays rendezvous parameters calculated by the LGC.
It will display Range, Range Rate and Theta.
The Range and Range Rate are calculated by the LGC using the stored LM and CSM state vectors.
The Theta is the angle between the LM +Z axis and the local horizontal plane at the current time, referenced to the direction of flight.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: In 1969, many of the features on the Moon's farside had yet to be identified and named by the International Astronomical Union (IAU).
The mare or basins were given Roman numerals as identifiers.
Smaller craters were identified using Arabic numerals.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford has misidentified a feature as crater 329.
Crater 329 is today know as Maunder and is situated within the northern half of Mare Orientale.
This crater is approximately 120° behind their current location.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater IX is now known as Mendeleev.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford has decided to amend the planned changes in the LM attitude to simplify matters and maintain an attitude which will allow strip photography.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: CSM solo operations detailed Flight Plan, page 30
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew are sharing the Hasselblad camera.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford wants the monocular for a close look at the features they are observing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is confirming that Stafford has decided to change the attitude they will be flying.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Using program 20, option 4, the CMC can point a specified spacecraft vector along the line of sight to the Lunar Module while constraining the spacecraft's rotation about this vector.
This option is used to acquire the Lunar Module in the sextant field of view and maintain the line of sight.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Both LM crew members are getting very excited about what they are seeing close up.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is referring to one of the rendezvous he and Stafford made with an ATDA (Augmented Target Docking Adapter) during Gemini GT-9A, in Earth orbit in 1966.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is referring to the ORDEAL FDAI 1 switch setting.
With it in the Inertial position, the CDR's FDAI will display the spacecraft's attitude with respect to the IMU.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The FDAI ball will be inverted.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan has now taken the monocular.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan wants to swap the Hasselblad camera for the monocular, so he can see in close up what Stafford is describing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Landing Radar circuit breaker on panel 11, will be closed at 22 minutes before the phasing manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan confirms he is leaving the 16-mm sequence camera running at 6 frames per second onto magazine G.
This includes an approach towards landing site 2
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 33 minutes, 31 seconds to phasing burn ignition.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 100 hours, 23 minutes.
We are just 1 minute away from acquisition ofCharlie Brown; 2 minutes, 23 seconds away from acquiringSnoopy.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Stafford is reminding himself that prior to the Landing Radar test, he has to pitch the LM up, so that the +X axis nearly corresponds to the local vertical, so as to present the Landing Radar antenna to the lunar surface.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is referring to Verb 64, which is used to initiate Routine 05 which is used to compute the S-Band steerable antenna angles which will point the antenna towards the centre of the Earth.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: How do you read high gain?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm all locked up on them, Tom, but I just don't read them.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: He'll fill you in when he gets to you.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: LMSnoopyshould now be passing over 100° east longitude, just east of Mare Smythii.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Very excited comments at seeing Earthrise.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: ...at 6 miles, he was doing 65 feet a second [20 m/s] on my - 6 miles [11 km] from me, he was doing 65 feet per second [20 m/s].
At 3.8 miles [7.0 km] he was doing 73 feet per second [22 m/s].
I think that confirms this burn.
They are down there among the rocks...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The 16mm camera with magazine D is quickly swung around to capture Earthrise.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford in a very annoyed tone, is referring to the Hasselblad camera.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is encouraging Stafford to try again a color magazine which has been malfunctioning.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: ...mumbling about the boulders and things right now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: They say they are looking up, looking up at the horizon now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We is Go, and we is down among them, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I hear you weaving your way up the freeway.
Can you give me a post-burn report?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: As soon as I get my breath.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Stafford is conceding that the malfunctioning color magazine on the Hasselblad camera isn't going work.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Our residuals were minus 0.1, minus 0.3 and minus 0.5.
And that was the residuals for the DPS burn.
We did not use the - We did not null anything out.
We are at 61.2 by 9.2 [nautical miles, 113.3 x 17.0 km] and the AGS has at an 8.6.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: DOI burn residual values are given in Roll, Pitch and Yaw in fps
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We copy all the residuals, and it looks like we are all Go.
Your DPS is looking good, and it's Go.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We just saw Earthrise and it's got to be magnificent.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, you can also tell Jack Schmitt...
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: ...that there are enough boulders around here to fill up Galveston Bay, too.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford is referring to scientist astronaut Harrison (Jack) Schmitt.
Schmitt was heavily involved in the geological training of the astronaut crews, and would eventually be the LMP on Apollo 17 in December 1972.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The only trouble is - We're stripping lots of film for him.
The spacecraft is looking good and there are no problems, Charlie, except it would be nice to be around here more often.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: All your systems are looking good to us.
Did you get your DPS pressure back?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's a negative, but the DPS burn was smoothed out very smooth, well when we throttled up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We got a good - good pressures here and the DPS is Go for phasing.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Can you image, no color film in that thing?
I ought to hit someone over the head with that -
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, I want to get it early.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I need - Have you got them locked on?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Go call P00 and then get us a Verb 83.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Let me run through - through 30 real quick.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan wants to confirm the configuration of P30 in preparation for the upcoming Phasing DPS burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: There will be no update on the phasing PAD.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'll be picking up our landing radar test and taking pictures here and it is a fantastic sight.
You do have different shades, of browns and grays here.
And right - the volcanism there is also a pure white near the edge, and the bottom is black, and we see some large boulders that are black to blackish gray.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Snoopyis over the Smyth's Sea area now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: OK, just wanted to check it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 27 minutes, 50 seconds to phasing burn ignition.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We've got 10 minutes to go before we get to Taruntius G and H.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Can you see them down there among the boulders?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I saw them about 14 miles [26 km].
But then I turned this thing over to CMC to let it do an Auto maneuver and it lost them.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I was tracking them just manually.
I'm going to let it update it with a little radar - a little range, and see if that brings it in.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The LM Landing Radar antenna can operate in two positions, position 1, descent or position 2 Hover.
Cernan is prompting the LR to go into the Hover position so that it can lock it's signal on to the lunar surface, 40,000 feet + (12,000 meters +) below.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As the landing radar has not yet locked on to the lunar surface, it has not transitioned yet from the Descent to the Hover antenna position.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: A little early, but let's get it in.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Should be reading about 42,000 feet [12,600 meters] when she actually locks on.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: A quarter of a degree down.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Why don't you just write it on there?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It looks like this landing radar is doing real good.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It looks to me we want to have a lock-on here, [garble] even early.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Approximately 25 minutes until the phasing burn ignition.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And straight up ahead, you can see the gulf from the highlands over to the maria area.
Just like you're crossing over to a black and gray sea.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Landing radar looks like it's locked on solid.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The landing radar antenna now has a lock-on to the lunar surface.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Beam acquisition occurred at 100:32:22, and the beams acquired tracker lock within 2 seconds of each other.
Slant range at acquisition was about 75,500 feet (23,000 meters), which corresponds to a true altitude above the surface of nearly 71,000 feet (21,650 meters).
Radar lock was maintained until an S-band communications problem caused loss of continuous downlink data at 100:36:32.

Sporadic data points were obtained until 100:41:43; at that time, the radar indicated a slant range of 50,460 feet (15,400 meters), equivalent to a true altitude of 47,400 feet (14,450 meters), or less than 8 miles (15 km) above the local surface.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I don't believe this thing's failed, do you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: What did that circuit breaker blew.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 23 minutes, I'm going to - 22:30, I'm going to connect the ascent batteries.
You got your land - PGNS Landing Radar breaker Closed, for sure?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 23 minutes until the phasing burn ignition.

Cernan is bringing the ascent stage batteries online, so that they can be monitored prior to the phasing burn maneuver, and available to power the spacecraft should an abort be necessary and the LM has to stage.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I got the ascent batteries connected at this time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I didn't realize there were so many things to do in such a short time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Give me that thing, Tom, if you can?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Snoopycoming up on 70 degrees east longitude now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Charlie, we're now about where the horizon is - almost, you know, level - at times seems a little bit high.
We're coming into - to a mare area 22 minutes before, before phasing right now.
The craters, the new craters, seem to throw out very, very light, light, almost whitish gray by comparison to the dirtier-color whites and grays and browns.
Right over the rim - there appears to be almost every fresh crater, there's visible boulders both down in it, on the sides and on the rim.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Also, Charlie, it looks like we're getting so close all you have to do is put your tail hook down and we're there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Air Force guys don't talk that way.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, I did fly an F4 that had one on it one time, even though it's an Air Force bird.
We're coming right over to the maria.
And it really smooth out here, comparatively speaking.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You should be starting to see Langrenus over there, T.P.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 21 minutes until the phasing burn ignition.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We have you; you should be at Taruntius very soon.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I've got them in the optics now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're at - I don't believe we're quite up to Taruntius yet.
We're in - we're in the Foaming Sea - We're past the Sea of Waves and in the Foaming Sea.
And the maria gets markedly smooth and flat.
There's small craters - a lot of small craters down in it, but the basic surface is extremely smooth.
And it appears that the craters go down into it, rather than - than the rims come up out of it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Foaming Sea-Mare Spumans, Sea of Waves-Mare Undarum.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You can tell that this area is definitely lower than that highland area, just as the whole general area.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I think right over the horizon, we've probably got Taruntius H and Taruntius G coming up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Could you come up on expected surface washout?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: When you look straight down, there's a definite three-dimensional effect where you can't really tell the depth of craters like we could in the past.
And in some cases, you're not even sure whether - whether there is depth to them except that they look a little familiar.
It's a little bit hazier and glarier.
It looks like we're right over a sub-solar point about now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The crews comments in the mission report indicate that the surface washout phenomenon was much less pronounced than had been expected.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Are they down there among them!
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Bet it looks like they're really hauling the mail.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Surprisingly enough, Charlie, it really doesn't look like we're moving too fast down here.
It's a very nice pleasant pace.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: In fact, it's exactly like when - tell Deke it's like you went out and practiced the other morning in Florida, as far as the relative bearing rate, it's about the same.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: A few days before the launch, Tom Stafford and Director of Flight Crew Operations, Deke Slayton, flew a T-38 jet over Florida for to simulate the angular bearing rate they would experience during the low LM pass.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Seems like we're coming up on my side on Taruntius G and I believe Tom's got his Taruntius H right there on his side.
These come out of the horizon differently.
They seem to come over the horizon and be much closer to you down here, than up there at 60 miles [111 km].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, again the craters in this area, are craters that are dug out of the surface and not craters that are throwing back from the surface.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It looks like we might be on the verge of [garble] loss of lock, here; I don't know.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The problems being encountered with the S-band steerable antenna, are due to the antenna not tracking the Earth.
The antenna is in a fixed position.
As the LM changes attitude the signal strength is gradually dropping.
The antenna was in a fixed position due to the antenna track-mode switch on panel 12, being moved from the Slew to Off position, rather than being placed in the Auto position.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Tom, I think you've either got Messier B or Secchi up there on your left in front of you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: If you read, Houston, we think your aerial is not tracking us too closely.
Could you pick us up on the signal strength?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: From this altitude, the maria still looks fairly smooth as far as overall approach characteristics coming into it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Snoopy, Houston said something about their signal strength or something.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Tell them again - tell me again what you want them to do there, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Apollo Ridge at 42 degree east longitude.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, if you read, we have Secchi on my right.
There's the first of the - here's Apollo Rille, right out the window!
Very, very easily distinguishable at this point, and it appears to be a couple of hundred feet deep.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Can you try to tweak up the high-gain signal strength?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'm not sure whether you're reading, but we're coming over the Apollo Ridge.
I just had Secchi on my right - Apollo Ridge appears to be a couple of hundred feet deep, but very smooth at the bottom.
We should have the Smokey Basin right out of Tom's window, on the left.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm on Omni, and we got good signal strength.
I don't know whether they're listening, but we're over Apollo Ridge and we're just passing Smokey Basin.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: If you read, have Snoopy tweak up the high gain.
We're not reading him at all.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie - Snoopy, Houston wants you to tweak up the high gain.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: OK, they should have it now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Houston, Snoopy said they can't hold you on the high gain.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Houston, we're on Omni and we just passed Mount Marilyn.
As a matter of fact, there's Weatherford Crater, I believe, right in here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Weatherford crater is named after Tom Stafford's home town in Oklahoma.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 14 minutes and 40 seconds until the phasing burn ignition.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're coming up on the Bear Mountain.
And you should have Maskelyne A.
Do you have a big crater, Maskelyne A, right there, right on the edge of our - right in front of you, down straight in front of you on the left?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We have Lonesome Mesa right here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Houston, I don't know whether you read or not, but that's the best we can do.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: They are still transmitting to you, Houston - but - they're reading you, Snoopy, and they're standing by.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're abeam of - we're abeam of Site 1.
Tom, give me that a minute.
let me see if I can take a couple of pictures.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I've got Censorinus A out here.
Hey, let me - I've got Censorinus A, right here, bigger than shit.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Hey, I tell you, we are low!
And it really looks pretty smooth down there, surprisingly enough.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Censorinus A has huge boulders all around the rim of it, falling on the inside and outside.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I've got Maskelyne out here off my right side.
We are coming up on Boot Hill which is very easy to distinguish, and Maskelyne.
And I see the craters that are going to lead us right into the - right into the landing site.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got Duke Island on the left, just past Boot Hill and we are coming up - I've got Wash Basin just off my right arm - very easily distinguishable.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Tom, ought to have Sidewinder Rille coming up on the left - Tom, give me that...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: What time did we pitch over, 11:40?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Diamondback Rille is very easy to see.
These rilles look like they may be as much as a couple of hundred feet deep and very smooth.
The surface actually looks very smooth, like a very wet clay, but smooth with the exception of the bigger craters.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, Tom, let me get a picture of those rilles!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Sidewinder Rille is round along the edges.
It is flat under, and smooth in the bottom.
The ridges are definitely rounded, and it doesn't look like the sides are upturned.
I've got Moltke up here on the left and we are coming into the site.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The best description I can give you of these rilles is of a dry - a dry riverbed out in New Mexico or Arizona someplace.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Here we are coming up on the site [garble] Moltke on the left.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: ...there's plenty of holes there, but there are a few smooth places.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We just went over crater number 12, we've got the landing site, Tom's got Moltke right out his left - right.
Take a picture of that, babe.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: almost like you could reach out and touch Moltke, and we're going just right over the landing site right this moment, and it's - a lot of - a lot of rounded-off craters, a few fresh ones, but basically, it looks pretty smooth, like a gummy grayish sand.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: My camera failed, I took so many pictures.
There's still lots of small craters down there, but I'd say you have about 30 percent of the area that looks free.
So if you've got Hover time, you can probably make it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Snoopy, Houston wants you to go to Downvoice Backup.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...I am, John, I'm in Downvoice Backup.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're just going over the landing site right now, and we should be coming up on Ritter - Sabine and Ritter and then Schmitt.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: US 1 has got a rounded side, and it looks like the whole thing is slumped down an even amount, like it is a huge graben.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: A graben is an area of surface that has dropped between two parallel faults.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Man, that must be - Tom, that must be seven, that must be a thousand feet wide.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: US 1 is a good - oh, almost a good mile - a thousand feet to a mile wide - even a couple thousand feet to a mile wide.
And the whole thing is slumped down.
And the plain is still pretty pock-marked with craters, but it looks like you could be able to set down there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'm so fed up with this stuff failing.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 16mm sequence camera film pack has failed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The attitude for the phasing manoeuvre as given in the PAD are, roll 000°, pitch 255° as displayed on the FDAI eight ball when it is in the Inertial mode.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We had a complete data dropout with Snoopy...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Pass it on: we got 9 minutes till burn; get in the attitude; and they're Go for phasing.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The DPS phasing burn puts the LM in a 'dwell' orbit which allows the CSM to overtake and pass the LM so that when the LM passes over landing site 2 on orbit 13, the LM will be trailing the CSM by approximately 27 nautical miles (50 km).
It will then be in the proper relative position for the LM insertion manoeuvre to simulate the ascent from the lunar surface during a landing mission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'm starting this right now, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Snoopy, Houston says you're Go for phasing.
Want to get you in the attitude.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You know this god-damned film pack failed on me?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, I tell you, babe, that's something.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Except I don't have any more [garble].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: What happened to my control systems?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're reading you about three-by now...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're counting 8 minutes to the burn.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And you're Go for the burn.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yes, You can't imagine the position we can see these things in, pitched way down like this.
It looks like we're not very far above them.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The LM has manoeuvred to the phasing attitude which has the crew facing down at the lunar surface, traveling heads first, ready for the posigrade phasing DPS burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You might try and get your helmets and gloves on.
I went through P30, and we're all good there again.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: OK, I thought we were pointing up in the air.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's hurry up and get this burn completed.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford is having problems donning his helmet.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We has been down among them, babe!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We got to start doing that earlier.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford feels they should have donned their helmet and gloves earlier.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew are preparing P40 to control the PNGS during the countdown to and performance of the Phasing DPS burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As part of P40, the LGC displays Verb 06 Noun 40 on the DSKY.

The display registers show the LGC calculated parameters:R1- TIG, time from DPS ignition, in minutes and seconds, to the nearest second, XX XX.R2- VG, the magnitude of the velocity to be gained in the thrusting manoeuvre, displayed in feet per second, to the nearest 0.1 fps.R3- Delta-VM, the measured Delta-V magnitude, in feet per second, to the nearest 0.1 fps.The values displayed on R3 will be shown at this time as 00000, and should not change until the ullage burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: They're going into a P40 now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Verb 70 - OK, you should have called a Verb 78 in there, babe.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Verb 78 is used to start Routine 77 which is used to readout the Range and Delta-V data from the Landing Radar.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Tom, the AGS is good for a takeover.
The AGS is good for a takeover.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me open and close this breaker.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got to get something out of this burn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: OK, Stab/Control: DECA Power, Closed; and AELD Closed.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Stab/Control: DECA Power Closed and AELD Closed.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: EPS Inverter number 1 Closed; I'm sorry.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: In case an abort and staging are required during the Phasing manoeuvre, the Electrical Power Subsystem (EPS) Inverter No 1 circuit breaker is closed so that by depressing the Abort Stage pushbutton on panel 1, will initiate a switchover from descent stage DC power to ascent stage AC power, prior to the physical separation of the stages.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: OK, Tom, if Delta-V to go is less than five (fps) (1.5 m/s), we'll RCS it to zero, 3 Max.
If Delta-V to go is less than 25 (fps) (7.6 m/s) but greater than 5 (fps) (1.5 m/s), we've got to stage in RCS to zero.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan is reading out the various off-nominal options if the Phasing DPS burn is not completed as planned, as specified in the Apollo 10 mission rules.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: If Delta-V to go is greater than 25 (fps) (7.6 m/s) we got to stage and APS to zero.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Now if we have to go to AGS, or APS, I've got the staging procedures and everything right here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: After - after burning - if we stage, we'll stage it and burn it in AGS and go.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: John, we'll go on VOX, so you can read it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We are being cut out, but I assume it's loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Coming up on 35 seconds: Engine Arm Descent?
Go to Auto on the AGS.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: DSKY blanks; hit the Proceed at 5 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: At DPS ignition minus 35 seconds the DSKY, Verb, Noun and all three register displays go blank for 5 seconds to indicate to the crew the PNGS was ready to ignite the DPS engine as specified.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Babe, the AGS is looking good, there is your AGS attitude...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: OK, and I'll trim out the burn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: OK, here we go for 26 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 5 seconds prior to the programmed DPS ignition time, a flashing 99 is displayed on the DSKY.
This is a prompt for the crew to confirm to the LGC that they wish to continue with the burn.
By pressing the Proceed key on the DSKY, the crew gives the LGC the instruction to commence the burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We've got an Engine Gimbal light, but everything is good.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The DPS engine uses gimbal drive actuators to trim the engine to ensure the thrust vector is acting through the LM's centre of mass and to assist in controlling the LM attitude.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: A master alarm and associated DPS engine pitch gimbal fail warning light were displayed during the phasing burn, when the DPS pitch gimbal actuator began 'coasting'.
Coasting is a uncommanded gimbal movement, which occurs when the spring brake mechanism fails to engage following the removal of drive signals.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: LM telemetry data indicate that the pitch and roll actuators both drove as expected.
The small number of thruster firings also demonstrate that thrust vector control was maintained using the actuators.
During the manoeuvre, the gimbal fail was indicated at the time of a reversal in pitch gimbal motion.
Coasting is not detrimental to the gimbal actuators operation.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Master Alarm warning light / push button is situated on panels 1 & 2 in front of both crewmen.
The ENG GMBL caution and warning light is situated at the top of panel 2.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: During the phasing burn, the DPS throttle at ignition is set at 13.1% for the first 26 seconds, then the throttle is advanced to 100% for the remaining ~17 seconds until the DPS cut-off.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 40 (fps, 12 m/s) to go, 40 (fps), 20 (fps, 6 m/s) to go.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's go to AGS Attitude Hold.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Once the AGS Attitude Hold mode is selected, the crew can command angular attitude changes at rates that are proportional to the deflection of the ACA (Attitude Control Assembly).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: minus point - plus 0.2, minus 0.5, and minus 0.9.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The residuals are the errors in each of the three axes, expressed in fps following the manoeuvre.
Cernan using the LM RCS will correct these errors.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Looked real good and the burn was steady, we had the Descent Quantity light on twice, we had the Engine Gimbal light on, the Master Warning and all those good things, but we just pressed right on.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: At ignition of the DPS for the Phasing burn, the Master Alarm was illuminated and the tone sounded.
It was drawing the crews attention to the illuminated DES QTY caution and warning light on panel 1.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Master Alarm was immediately reset.
The low level indication started at 23 minutes prior to the Phasing manoeuvre, as indicated by telemetry.
It remained in this state from this time and through the Phasing DPS burn.
The low level indication was probably caused by a gas bubble, which uncovered the low level sensor.
This then caused the low level light to latch on, but not to illuminate until the DPS engine firing circuit was enabled at Phasing ignition.
Hence only at ignition of the DPS, did the Master Alarm and DES QTY caution and warning lights appear.
The low level sensor remains on even after the Master Alarm was reset.
It will only go off if the PQGS (Propellant Quantity Gauging System) circuit breaker is cycled OFF and then back ON.
However on this occasion the DES QTY C&W light did go out, which is contrary to the system design.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, we are in a 190.8 by 11.8 [nautical miles, 353.4 x 21.8 km].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You can - Let me take my helmet and gloves off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me get the DECA Gimbal open.

tokens: And we'll get the Landing Radar.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: MCC-H has now begun to receive the LR data via the telemetry downlink.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Oh, I guess it's just does.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The comm is really great and we got all our data now.
During the good part though, boy you were way down in the mud, but everything is copacetic now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I fought with the S-band antenna to get it for you, but it's the best I could do and I got the strongest strength Omni when we were down there.
And then after we passed, I gave S-band another try and we came in good, because I thought you'd want to see the burn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: During the beginning of revolution 13, the S-band steerable antenna did not properly track.
At Acquisition Of Signal, the received signal strength at the ground station indicated near-boresight condition for the antenna.
Over the next 13 minutes, the signal strength gradually decreased 20 dB.
A plot of expected signal strength below, considering spacecraft attitude changes and antenna gain patterns, showed that the antenna was not moving at this time.
The antenna performed well both before and after this period.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The possible causes for failure of the antenna to move were either that the servo system circuit was open or the antenna track-mode switch was in the Slew or Off position.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The track-mode switch for the steerable antenna on panel 12, is a three-position switch (down - Slew; centre - Off; up - Auto).
The crew reported that the switch may have inadvertently been switched to Off instead of to Auto at the time acquisition had been established.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: At this time, Young is using P76 to update the CMC LM state vector following the Phasing manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You'd like to know that we have taken so many pictures that both cameras have failed on us.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The 16-mm sequence camera had not actually failed, but Cernan continued to have problems getting the film cassette to engage properly.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: OK, Tom, we want to do some tracking.
You got your - you got this yaw maneuver, put your Rendezvous Radar breaker In.

tokens: The Rendezvous Radar is powered up again.

And you're probably in - Oh, you're in inertial now, aren't you?

transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The above PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:Backup insertion.Systems:The burn will be made using the large SPS (Service Propulsion System) engine, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):102:58:01.00Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):x, +170.0 (51.8); y, +000.0 (0.0); z, -71.0 (21.6)Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 180°; Pitch 262°; Yaw 2°.
The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform.Delta-VC:171.8 fps (52.4 m/s).
This value is the change in velocity that MCC expect the EMS to sense.Ullage using the plus-X thruster on each of the four RCS quads for 10 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: See if you can get him.

transcript.speaker: Cernan

transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Did you take a look at 06 49 and see what we got there?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Whilst running P20 to take navigation marks on the CSM using the rendezvous radar, the crew key Verb 06 Noun 49 on the DSKY to display the Delta-R (range), XXXX.X nm, and Delta-V (rate of closure), XXXX.X fps.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On each occasion, when the crew ran P20 to take rendezvous radar (RR) navigation marks, the initial LGC readout of range rate from the RR is displayed as a set of random and very large values.
This readout is what Stafford is drawing to the attention of MCC-H.
This erroneous readout resulted in the state vector update being loaded in to the LGC erasable memory (e-memory) exceeded the allowable limits.
The flashing Verb 06 Noun 49 display these large values on the DSKY, and can be discarded and cleared from the DSKY by entering Verb 32 Enter.
This causes the RR data Read routine to recycle and bring forth new values.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The large values initially displayed are the result of the RR output shift register, which is not rest to zero when it is powered up.
The initial bit configuration is unpredictable, although due to the design of the register the output is predominantly a series of ones.
This initial bit configuration is subsequently passed to the LGC as a range rate value, when the first readout command is sent from the LGC to the RR (Verb 06 Noun 49).
Once this initial bit register has been passed to the LGC, the RR output register is cleared and repopulated by valid data from the radar.
Therefore the initial radar data displayed on the DSKY give a valid range value but an invalid range rate value.
All subsequent marks will be accurate.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: A procedural change was made for Apollo 11 to alleviate this problem.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The first one was a small number: 0.4 and zero.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Did we put it - no, not - don't let that son of a bitch update the vector.
We've got a Verb 95 in it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Whilst running P20, it is possible to use the acquired navigation data to update the LM state vector by keying Verb 80 Enter, update the CSM state vector by keying Verb 81 Enter, or to update neither in the LGC, key Verb 95 Enter.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It looks like big numbers now.

transcript.speaker: Cernan

transcript.speaker: Duke

transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Your insertion PAD: TIG, starting with TIG- 102:55:01.40, Minus 01832, plus all balls, minus 0123.5.
Delta-VRis 02209, 015, minus - correction, roll is 180, 233, minus 01818, plus all balls, minus 01255, rest of the PAD is NA.

The change in velocity is resolved into three components expressed relative to the 'Local Vertical' frame of reference.Total required change in velocity, Delta-VR, fps (m/s):220.9 (67.3)Burn duration, BT (min:sec):0:15Attitude angles that should be displayed on the FDAI eight ball in the Inertial mode:Roll 180°, Pitch 233°Change in velocity values used to target the AGS (Noun 86), fps (m/s):X -181.8 (55.4), Y -000.0 (0.0), Z -125.5 (38.3).These velocities are expressed relative to the Local Vertical frame of reference.

These angles are displayed on the DSKY registers whilst Verb 06 Noun 93 flashes.

tokens: Stafford is confirming that he is in Direct-Attitude Control position.
When in PGNS, Direct mode can be used in any axis.
Displacing the ACA a quarter of a full throw, causes continuous two-jet RCS firing for the selected axes.
The acceleration induced by this firing has to be manually nulled, to stop the motion.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now on the Orb-rate ball at this time.
I guess we're about 150 miles from John.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I guess we are about 150 miles from you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And our P50 - our P52 is complete.
We're coming over to start tracking on you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Did you get your AOS/LOS times?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And we have lost the signal onCharlie Brown.
About 40 seconds away from losing the signal withSnoopy.
AndSnoopyhas gone behind the Moon now on the 13th lunar revolution.
He reported thatSnoopyhad reported the DOI burn and that he felt it was a good one.

And then whenSnoopycame within range, Tom Stafford and Gene Cernan confirmed that the Descent Orbit Insertion burn had been on time and they were reading an onboard orbit of 61.2 by 9.2 nautical miles [113.3 x 27.4 km].
They were down among the boulders, obviously very impressed at what they were seeing.
Communications were bad a large part of this pass as we lost lock on the high gain antenna.
Then shortly before the phasing burn, you could hear Gene Cernan calling out the checklist for the burn to Tom Stafford.
They were on what is called VOX - voice operated circuits - talking to each other on board.
We had a good phasing burn.
This maneuver coming slightly more than 10 minutes afterSnoopyhad passed over landing site 2.
He reported that both cameras aboardSnoopyhad failed during this low pass.
This phasing maneuver will putSnoopybehind and belowCharlie Brown.
The Descent Orbit Insertion had placedSnoopyout ahead.
Now they've burned into an orbit with an apocynthion of 190 nautical miles [352 km] and a pericynthion of 11.8 nautical miles [21.9 km].

SoSnoopywill start dropping behind and will go behind and belowCharlie Brownto reach the position where it will have the same lighting and the same position as the Lunar Module on Apollo 11 will have after lift-off from the lunar surface.
This phasing maneuver was done at 10% throttle for the first 26 seconds and then full throttle.
You heard Gene Cernan call off the numbers of the Delta-V as it burned down.
Acquisition forCharlie Brownwill be at 102 hours, 22 minutes, 22 seconds and forSnoopy; 102 hours, 25 minutes, 37 seconds; not quite 45 minutes from now.
The next maneuver to be performed will be ranging which will come near 102 hours, 45 minutes.
We have tape of a briefing by Pete Frank, Flight Director on the shift preceding this shift and there has been a request that we play that on the release line.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: During the low-altitude pass, the Lunar Module 16-mm camera failed to operate with magazine F installed.
Magazine F was replaced immediately with magazine G, and the camera operated satisfactorily.
Magazine F was reinstalled later for staging and the terminal phase of the rendezvous, and the camera operated satisfactorily.
Proper alignment of the camera and the magazine required greater care with magazine F because of marginal clearances at the interface surfaces and edges.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I can't hold my - the hose is floating up, you know it?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The crew reported that the Lunar Module drinking water contained gas.
The nitrogen used to pressurize the water system permeates the tank bladder, and the water becomes saturated within 100 hours after servicing.
As the absolute pressure is reduced the dissolved nitrogen is released.
The first water withdrawn should have contained about 12 percent of gas.
At staging, the mixture should have contained 6.3 percent nitrogen because of the reduced water tank pressure at that time.
The water hose, water gun, and connecting plumbing were not serviced and this entrapped air would initially add to the problem.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Let's see, we've got to get some tracking on this guy.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's the trouble with these darn air vents.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Bubbles are present in the water that Cernan has injected in to his food pack.

tokens: What do we have in the way of 70 millimeter film?
Jack was saying we could strip the back side of that area...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Just as I was - That's what I was bitching about: the Hasselblad burned out as I hit the landing site!
That's why I was so fucking mad.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: During the low-altitude lunar pass, the Hasselblad 70-mm camera, which had the last magazine installed, stopped because of film binding in the magazine.
The binding resulted from internal damage to the film advance mechanism, including burrs on the film guide.
The emulsion scraped from the film by the burrs built up on the rollers, decreasing the clearance.
This condition continuously overloaded the drive motor until the motor failed approximately five frames from the end of film.
The 1.6-ampere fuse in the camera would have protected the motor against a direct short, but not against a continuous overload.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On one of my magazines, you have to ja - Look at, even now, you got to jam it in.
Light goes out - there it goes.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan is pointing out that the Ascent Propulsion System (APS) engine burn at Insertion will be far more obvious than the acceleration from the DPS during the DOI and Phasing burns.
This is due to the far lower mass of the LM now that the descent stage has been separated from.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Rendezvous Radar (RR) mode select switch on panel 3 is placed in the Auto Track position.
In this position the RR maintains track of the CSM transponder by comparing the received radar signals from the three channels.
The errors are used to drive the radar servos.
Once the RR is in this mode, the crew call up P20 again to take navigation marks on the CSM using the RR data, to update the LM or CSM state vector in the LGC.
transcript.speaker: Commentary
